title: How much does a concrete driveway cost in 2026?
excerpt: A new concrete driveway runs $4-$15 per square foot installed in 2026 — typical 2-car driveways land between $3,500 and $7,800.
metaKeywords: concrete driveway price per square foot
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for concrete projects. It tracks installed driveway, patio, slab, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A standard broom-finish concrete driveway in 2026 lands between $4 and $9 per square foot installed, with the national typical falling near $6.25 per square foot for a 4-inch slab on a prepared subgrade. That figure is normalized for 4,000 PSI ready-mix delivery, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers, expansion joints at 10-foot intervals, and a single mobilization fee. Decorative finishes — stamped, exposed aggregate, integral color — push the per-square-foot rate to $10-$15. Removal and haul-off of an existing slab adds $2-$4 per square foot on top. The single biggest swing factor on a homeowner's actual bill is subgrade prep, which can shift the total by 15-30 percent depending on soil and slope.

faqs.question: How much does a 2-car concrete driveway cost in 2026?
faqs.answer: A typical 2-car concrete driveway (roughly 16 by 40 feet, or 640 square feet) costs $3,500 to $7,800 installed in 2026. The national typical sits near $4,000 for a basic broom finish at $6.25 per square foot. Higher-end stamped or colored finishes on the same footprint run $6,400 to $9,600. The single biggest variable is whether the existing driveway needs to be removed first, which adds about $1,280 to $2,560 to the project.

faqs.question: What is the average price per square foot for a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: The national average for a poured concrete driveway in 2026 is $6.25 per square foot installed for a standard 4-inch broom-finish slab. Real installed prices range from $4 per square foot in low-cost Southern and Midwest markets up to $15 per square foot for decorative finishes in coastal metros. That price includes ready-mix delivery, labor, light grading, #4 rebar, and basic expansion joints — but not demo, deep excavation, or steep-slope drainage work.

faqs.question: Is a concrete driveway more expensive than asphalt or pavers?
faqs.answer: Concrete sits in the middle of the driveway pricing spectrum in 2026. Pavers run $15-$30 per square foot installed, concrete runs $4-$15, and the lower end of concrete pricing is competitive with mid-grade gravel and chip-seal. Over a 30-year lifecycle, concrete typically costs less per year than alternatives because resurfacing and sealcoating cycles are longer and replacement frequency is lower.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete driveway be?
faqs.answer: A residential concrete driveway should be a minimum of 4 inches thick for passenger vehicles, and 5-6 inches thick if it will support a pickup, SUV, RV, or boat trailer. According to the Portland Cement Association, going from 4 inches to 5 inches roughly increases load capacity by 50 percent. Each additional inch of thickness adds about $0.75-$1.25 per square foot to the installed price.

faqs.question: How long does a concrete driveway last?
faqs.answer: A properly installed concrete driveway lasts 30-50 years before requiring full replacement. The American Concrete Institute reports that concrete with a water-cement ratio at or below 0.45, proper air entrainment for freeze-thaw climates, and correctly spaced control joints regularly exceeds 40 years of service. Cracking, spalling, or settlement within the first 5-10 years typically points to subgrade or mix-design failure, not normal wear.

faqs.question: Do I need rebar in a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: Reinforcement is strongly recommended on driveways over 100 square feet. Standard residential practice in 2026 is #4 rebar on 18-inch centers, or 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh, set at mid-slab depth. Fiber-mesh reinforcement adds about $0.25 per square foot but does not replace steel for structural load resistance — it primarily reduces plastic shrinkage cracking during curing.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to remove an old concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: Removal and haul-off of an existing concrete driveway runs $2-$4 per square foot in 2026, or roughly $1,280-$2,560 on a typical 2-car driveway. The price depends on slab thickness, whether the slab is reinforced, disposal fees in the local market, and access for breaking equipment. Removal is typically billed separately from the new pour, so always ask whether a quote includes demo.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest way to get a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: The lowest legitimate installed price is a 4-inch broom-finish slab on a flat, well-drained lot with no demolition required — that combination lands near $4 per square foot in low-cost markets. Quotes below $4 per square foot usually mean the contractor is skipping rebar, expansion joints, proper subgrade compaction, or using under-spec'd 3,000 PSI mix. Each of those shortcuts shows up as cracking, settlement, or spalling within 3-7 years.

howToSteps.name: Stake and lay out the driveway
howToSteps.text: The contractor marks the perimeter with stakes and string, confirms the final dimensions match the quote, and verifies slope away from any structure. Minimum slope is 1 percent for drainage, typical is 2 percent.

howToSteps.name: Excavate and prepare the subgrade
howToSteps.text: Topsoil and organic material is stripped to 6-8 inches below final grade. The exposed subgrade is graded for proper fall, then compacted with a plate compactor or jumping jack. Soft spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.

howToSteps.name: Install gravel base and forms
howToSteps.text: A 4-inch compacted layer of crushed stone or 57 stone is installed as the base. 2x4 or 2x6 dimensional lumber forms are staked along the perimeter to set the slab edges and final elevation.

howToSteps.name: Place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: #4 rebar on 18-inch centers or 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh is set on chairs at mid-slab depth. Bar should be tied at intersections and lapped a minimum of 12 inches.

howToSteps.name: Pour and screed the concrete
howToSteps.text: 4,000 PSI ready-mix concrete is placed from the truck chute or pumped in. The crew screeds with a straight edge across the forms, then bull-floats to bring the cream to the surface.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints and finish
howToSteps.text: Control joints are cut at intervals roughly 24-30 times the slab thickness (10-foot spacing on a 4-inch slab). A broom finish is applied perpendicular to the direction of travel for traction. Decorative finishes such as stamping or exposed aggregate happen at this stage.

howToSteps.name: Cure the slab
howToSteps.text: A curing compound is sprayed on the surface, or the slab is covered with plastic and kept damp for 7 days. Vehicle traffic should stay off for 7 days minimum, and heavy loads should wait 28 days for full design strength.

content:
Quick Answer: A new concrete driveway costs $4 to $15 per square foot installed in 2026. National typical for a standard 2-car broom-finish driveway is $6.25 per square foot, landing most projects between $3,500 and $7,800. Stamped or decorative finishes push the total to $6,400-$9,600. Removal of an existing slab adds $2-$4 per square foot.

Concrete driveways are the most-quoted residential concrete project in the United States — and also the project where homeowners get burned the most often on opaque pricing. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting, so the dollar figures on every page reflect what the slab actually cost to put in the ground. This guide breaks the 2026 numbers down so a homeowner can read a quote, spot what is missing, and know exactly where the negotiable line items sit.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for concrete projects. It tracks installed driveway, patio, slab, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A standard broom-finish concrete driveway in 2026 lands between $4 and $9 per square foot installed, with the national typical falling near $6.25 per square foot for a 4-inch slab on a prepared subgrade. That figure is normalized for 4,000 PSI ready-mix delivery, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers, expansion joints at 10-foot intervals, and a single mobilization fee. Decorative finishes — stamped, exposed aggregate, integral color — push the per-square-foot rate to $10-$15. Removal and haul-off of an existing slab adds $2-$4 per square foot on top. The single biggest swing factor on a homeowner's actual bill is subgrade prep, which can shift the total by 15-30 percent depending on soil and slope.

National cost ranges by size and finish

A concrete driveway price is built up from four line items: ready-mix delivery, labor, prep (excavation + subgrade + base), and finish upcharges. Two driveways with identical square footage can land $3,000 apart based on prep depth and finish choice alone. The table below shows 2026 national ranges for the most common driveway sizes, in standard 4-inch broom finish.

SizeSquare feetLowNational typicalHigh
1-car (10' x 20')200 sq ft$800$1,250$1,800
2-car (16' x 40')640 sq ft$2,560$4,000$5,760
2-car wide (20' x 40')800 sq ft$3,200$5,000$7,200
3-car (30' x 40')1,200 sq ft$4,800$7,500$10,800
Long single (10' x 60')600 sq ft$2,400$3,750$5,400

The same 2-car driveway in a stamped or stained finish lands between $6,400 and $9,600. Exposed aggregate sits between broom and stamped, usually $7 to $11 per square foot. According to the Portland Cement Association, decorative finishes don't change the structural performance of the slab — the underlying mix design and reinforcement are identical to a broom finish — so the upcharge is entirely labor and color material. Homeowners on the national directory at best concrete contractors consistently report that stamped finishes are also where contractors quote with the widest variance, often a 40-50 percent spread on the same scope.

What drives the price of a concrete driveway

Five inputs explain almost every dollar of variance on a residential driveway quote. In rough order of impact on the final bill: subgrade prep, slab thickness, finish type, reinforcement, and mobilization. A homeowner who understands what each contributes can read a quote in under 60 seconds.

Subgrade prep

Subgrade is the soil under the slab. If it is sandy, well-drained, and already at grade, prep is light — a few hours of grading and a pass with a plate compactor. If it is clay-heavy, slopes more than 5 percent, or sits on uncompacted fill, the contractor has to dig deeper, swap in compacted gravel, and possibly install drainage. Subgrade work can shift a driveway bill by 15-30 percent. Quotes that don't mention excavation depth, base material, or compaction method are almost always under-specifying this line item.

Slab thickness

Standard residential thickness is 4 inches. Heavier-duty applications — RV pads, trailer storage, work trucks — call for 5-6 inches. According to the American Concrete Institute, slab thickness has a non-linear relationship to load capacity: going from 4 inches to 5 inches increases load capacity by roughly 50 percent, not 25 percent. Each extra inch adds $0.75 to $1.25 per square foot. On a 2-car driveway that is $480-$800 to upgrade to 5 inches.

Finish type

Broom finish is the default and the cheapest. Trowel finish is too slick for driveways — it's a patio or interior finish. Exposed aggregate, stamped, and integral-colored finishes add $3-$8 per square foot. The upcharge covers extra labor (additional crew hours during finishing), specialty equipment (stamping mats, release agent), and color material.

Reinforcement

The American Concrete Institute calls for reinforcement on residential driveways over 100 square feet. The two common options are #4 rebar on 18-inch centers or 6-by-6 welded wire mesh. Rebar adds about $0.60 per square foot to the slab cost. Fiber-mesh additive adds $0.25 per square foot but does not replace steel for structural reinforcement — it controls plastic shrinkage cracking during curing, nothing more. Quotes that list "fiber mesh" instead of rebar or WWM are skipping the structural reinforcement.

Mobilization and access

Every concrete pour carries a mobilization fee — the truck, pump if needed, the crew getting to the site, and any minimum-yard surcharge from the ready-mix supplier. On a small driveway, mobilization can be 20-30 percent of the total. The fee shrinks as a share of the bill as the pour gets bigger, which is why doubling the driveway size does not double the cost.

How to read a concrete driveway quote

A complete concrete driveway quote will name every one of these items: square footage, slab thickness, PSI of the ready-mix, reinforcement type, joint spacing, finish, demolition scope (if any), and a payment schedule. Quotes that compress those into a single "installed price" lump are not necessarily dishonest, but the homeowner has no way to compare them against other quotes line for line.

The most common gaps in a residential driveway quote are: no PSI listed (assume 3,000 unless stated otherwise — that is under-spec for a driveway), no reinforcement listed (assume none), no demolition allowance (gets billed as a change order), and no control-joint spacing (cracking is then on the homeowner to absorb). According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, the minimum recommended mix for residential driveways is 4,000 PSI with air entrainment in any region that sees a freeze-thaw cycle.

How a concrete driveway gets installed

The pour itself is one day. The full project is two to five days depending on demo, weather, and curing. The steps below match what a professional crew will execute on a standard residential driveway.

  1. Stake and lay out the driveway. The contractor marks the perimeter with stakes and string, confirms the final dimensions match the quote, and verifies slope away from any structure. Minimum slope is 1 percent for drainage, typical is 2 percent.
  2. Excavate and prepare the subgrade. Topsoil and organic material is stripped to 6-8 inches below final grade. The exposed subgrade is graded for proper fall, then compacted with a plate compactor or jumping jack. Soft spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.
  3. Install gravel base and forms. A 4-inch compacted layer of crushed stone or 57 stone is installed as the base. 2x4 or 2x6 dimensional lumber forms are staked along the perimeter to set the slab edges and final elevation.
  4. Place reinforcement. #4 rebar on 18-inch centers or 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh is set on chairs at mid-slab depth. Bar should be tied at intersections and lapped a minimum of 12 inches.
  5. Pour and screed the concrete. 4,000 PSI ready-mix concrete is placed from the truck chute or pumped in. The crew screeds with a straight edge across the forms, then bull-floats to bring the cream to the surface.
  6. Cut control joints and finish. Control joints are cut at intervals roughly 24-30 times the slab thickness (10-foot spacing on a 4-inch slab). A broom finish is applied perpendicular to the direction of travel for traction. Decorative finishes such as stamping or exposed aggregate happen at this stage.
  7. Cure the slab. A curing compound is sprayed on the surface, or the slab is covered with plastic and kept damp for 7 days. Vehicle traffic should stay off for 7 days minimum, and heavy loads should wait 28 days for full design strength.

Stamped vs broom vs exposed aggregate pricing

Finish is the single discretionary line item where homeowners can move the price the most. The decision is essentially aesthetic — every finish below is built on the same 4-inch reinforced slab, so structural performance is identical.

Finish2026 price per square footUpcharge vs broomTypical use case
Broom finish$4 - $9baselinestandard residential driveway
Exposed aggregate$7 - $11+$2 - $3upgrade from broom, slip resistance
Integral color (broom)$5 - $10+$1 - $1.50cheap visual upgrade
Stamped (single color)$10 - $14+$5 - $6cobblestone, slate, brick patterns
Stamped (multi-color)$12 - $15+$7 - $8realistic stone, antiqued finishes

Stamped concrete on a driveway adds $3,200-$5,000 to a typical 2-car project. If the homeowner is debating stamped vs pavers on the same scope, pavers run $15-$30 per square foot installed — so stamped concrete is often the budget alternative to a true paver driveway with a similar visual. For service-specific pricing detail, the stamped and decorative concrete hub on Cost of Concrete tracks per-pattern price ranges by region.

When repair is cheaper than replacement

Not every cracked or settled driveway needs to be torn out. Repair is the right call when the structural slab is intact and the failure is surface-level or settlement-only. According to the Federal Highway Administration guidance on rigid pavement preservation, slabs with cracks under 1/4 inch wide, no rocking at the joints, and no spalling at the edges can usually be repaired and resealed for 20-40 percent of replacement cost.

Common repairs and their 2026 pricing: crack injection ($8-$15 per linear foot), polyjacking or mudjacking to lift a settled slab ($3-$8 per square foot), joint resealing ($1-$3 per linear foot), and surface resurfacing or microtopping ($3-$7 per square foot). The Cost of Concrete concrete repair service hub has the full repair cost breakdown. Full replacement only makes economic sense when the slab is structurally compromised, settling differentially, or has spalling across more than 30 percent of the surface.

Regional and seasonal price variation

Concrete driveway prices vary 30-50 percent between U.S. metros. The cheapest installed prices in 2026 are in the Midwest and lower South — Indianapolis, Kansas City, Memphis, Birmingham — where ready-mix is locally produced and labor markets are softer. The most expensive markets are Northeast and West Coast metros — Boston, Seattle, San Francisco, New York — where mix-design requirements for freeze-thaw plus higher labor rates can push installed prices 40-60 percent above the national typical.

Within a single market, season matters. Concrete crews are busiest April through October. Quotes pulled in November-February in non-freezing markets often run 5-10 percent lower because the crew has open capacity. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency stormwater management guidance, contractors will sometimes also recommend pervious concrete in stormwater-impacted regions, which runs $7-$12 per square foot and has different installation requirements. Homeowners shopping by metro can compare local pricing on the national best concrete contractors directory or jump straight to a city listicle like Mooresville, NC for regional examples.

Frequently asked questions

A typical 2-car concrete driveway (roughly 16 by 40 feet, or 640 square feet) costs $3,500 to $7,800 installed in 2026. The national typical sits near $4,000 for a basic broom finish at $6.25 per square foot. Higher-end stamped or colored finishes on the same footprint run $6,400 to $9,600. The single biggest variable is whether the existing driveway needs to be removed first, which adds about $1,280 to $2,560 to the project.

That price includes ready-mix delivery, labor, light grading, #4 rebar, and basic expansion joints — but not demo, deep excavation, or steep-slope drainage work.

Each additional inch of thickness adds about $0.75-$1.25 per square foot to the installed price.

Fiber-mesh reinforcement adds about $0.25 per square foot but does not replace steel for structural load resistance — it primarily reduces plastic shrinkage cracking during curing.

Key takeaways

  • National typical for a 2-car broom-finish concrete driveway in 2026 is $4,000, with a real range of $3,500-$7,800 depending on prep and finish.
  • Per-square-foot installed pricing runs $4-$9 for broom, $7-$11 for exposed aggregate, and $10-$15 for stamped.
  • Subgrade prep, slab thickness, and finish type drive most of the variance — not the contractor's hourly rate.
  • 4,000 PSI ready-mix, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers, and 10-foot control joint spacing are the minimum residential specs to expect on a 2026 quote.
  • Demolition of an existing slab is a separate $1,280-$2,560 line item on a typical 2-car driveway and is often missing from initial quotes.
  • Repair, not replacement, is usually the right call when cracks are under 1/4 inch, no rocking at the joints, and no edge spalling.

Need a contractor whose quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or jump to your city's page to compare local installed prices line by line against the national ranges above.
